comments.text: Quick clarification question: my colleague has mentioned that Erdos's probabilistic bound $R(k)\gg 2^{k/2}$ can be derandomized via the method of conditional expectations, yielding a deterministic construction of a 2-coloring of $K_n$ with no monochromatic $K_k$ for $n\approx 2^{k/2}$. Is such a derandomization considered "constructive" for the purposes of this problem, or would it make sense to clarify the question to ask for a substantially more explicit/efficient/algebraic construction (e.g. a fixed family of graphs)?

Explicit. The derandomization here only gives an existence proof. For $n$ large it would indeed not be efficient either. An algebraic construction would be good, but other ways of constructing would be allowed as well.

The term "constructive" is somewhat subjective in mathematics, but perhaps one concrete question would be to exhibit a deterministic algorithm that, for a given $k$, produces a graph on exponentially many vertices with Ramsey number at least $k$, in time that is guaranteed to be less than double exponential in $k$.

Gpt 5.5 pro seems to have found a solution to this formulation Given \(k\), there is a deterministic algorithm running in \(2^{O(k^2)}\) time which outputs a graph on \(2^{\Omega(k)}\) vertices with no clique or independent set of size \(k\). Let\[ N=\lfloor 2^{k/3}\rfloor,\qquad r=\binom{k}{2}. \]Fix once and for all a lexicographic ordering of the edges of \(K_N\). For a partial red/blue colouring \(P\) of the edges of \(K_N\), define\[ \Phi(P)=\sum_{S\in\binom{[N]}{k}} \Pr(S\text{ becomes monochromatic after completing }P\text{ uniformly at random}). \]This quantity is exactly computable by enumerating all \(k\)-sets \(S\). For a given \(S\), the summand is \(0\) if \(S\) already contains both a red and a blue edge; it is \(2^{-u}\) if \(S\) has at least one already-coloured edge, all already-coloured edges of \(S\) have the same colour, and \(u\) edges of \(S\) remain uncoloured; and it is \(2^{1-r}\) if all \(r\) edges of \(S\) remain uncoloured. Initially\[ \Phi(\varnothing) = \binom{N}{k}2^{1-r} \leq \frac{N^k}{k!}2^{1-k(k-1)/2} \leq \frac{2^{k^2/3+1-k(k-1)/2}}{k!} = \frac{2^{1-k^2/6+k/2}}{k!} <1 \]for all sufficiently large \(k\). Now colour the edges one at a time in the fixed order. Suppose \(e\) is the next uncoloured edge. Then\[ \Phi(P)=\frac12\Phi(P\cup\{e=\mathrm{red}\}) +\frac12\Phi(P\cup\{e=\mathrm{blue}\}), \]so at least one of the two choices keeps \(\Phi\) from increasing. Choose such a colour, breaking ties by choosing red. This is the standard method of conditional expectations. After all edges have been coloured, \(\Phi(P)\) is exactly the number of monochromatic \(K_k\)'s in the final colouring. Since \(\Phi\) never increased and \(\Phi(\varnothing)<1\), this final number is a nonnegative integer less than \(1\), hence it is \(0\). Thus the red graph has neither a \(k\)-clique nor a \(k\)-independent set. The running time is \(2^{O(k^2)}\). There are \(O(N^2)=2^{O(k)}\) edge decisions, and for each decision one may recompute \(\Phi\) by enumerating\[ \binom{N}{k}\leq N^k=2^{O(k^2)} \]\(k\)-sets and inspecting \(O(k^2)\) edges. All probabilities can be handled exactly, for instance by scaling \(\Phi\) by \(2^r\) and working with integers. Finally, since\[ N=\lfloor 2^{k/3}\rfloor\geq 2^{k/4} \]for all sufficiently large \(k\), this deterministic algorithm outputs a graph on at least \(2^{k/4}\) vertices with no clique or independent set of size \(k\). Hence it proves \(R(k)>2^{k/4}\) for all sufficiently large \(k\), in the concrete algorithmic sense, with running time well below double-exponential in \(k\). Equivalently, after decreasing the base constant if desired, this gives \(R(k)>C^k\) for some absolute constant \(C>1\).

Yes, this is the well-known method of conditional expectations. (The method is detailed for example in the book of Alon and Spencer, and I'm sure that applying it to $R(k)$ like this has been written up explicitly a number of times before.) What is usually meant by 'explicit' nowadays (see for example the introduction of [Co15], cited in the comments) is that there is an algorithm which, given any two vertices, determines whether the edge between them is present, which runs in time polynomial in $\log n$ (if the graph has $n$ vertices).
